speaker: interviewer
text: The oral history with Mr. Haller in Flushing, New York. The interview continues to be in English, and we are going to start tape number five. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As we left off tape number four, Mr Haller, you were telling us about the reunion of Holocaust survivors in 1981 in Israel and your attendance there with your wife. Will you please continue with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this-- this world gathering was, for us survivors, a-- actually a-- a relief. It-- it-- it-- it gave us the opportunity to speak about-- about our suffering that we had during the times of the Holocaust period, and we-- we saw other friends of ours. We saw the-- the state of Israel, what they had done in the state of Israel. We saw-- we saw the kibbutz-- several of the kibbutzim that were-- that were built by survivors, and we saw what they had accomplished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and before we left for the-- for the gathering, when we were still here at home, they had asked us to make a tape-- a audiotape and tell in that tape all the history what had happened to us during the camp, and I did this. And this tape was sent-- was given to Yad Vashem. And because of that, it really-- it was a relief to all-- to all of us. I know many of the survivors that have never spoken about the-- about the history of what had happened, now opened up and talk freely about it. So then when we came back from Israel, in our-- in our synagogue we had-- every year, they had a Yom HaShoah on the day of the Remembrance Day, our synagogue used to go to another synagogue to join the-- to join the-- the event over there, to commemorate the event. So when we came back from Israel, I said-- I said to the rabbi, I said, look, we-- we do not have to go to another synagogue to do this. We have, in our congregation, about 30 families that are survivors, and we can do our own program. So of course, right away, they elected me to be the chairman of the program. So for the last 13 years, I have-- I have been the chairman of this program. We have a Holocaust Memorial Committee in our synagogue, and we speak about it. I became a member of the Queensborough Community Resource Center-- the Holocaust Resource Center, where I am on their speaker forum, where I go to-- to schools and organizations that request speakers, that want to know-- to learn about the Holocaust. And once in a while, they call me and send me out to these places, and I speak freely about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before that, I was not able to do it. For instance, my children, I-- I was trying to tell them-- in the beginning, I was trying to tell them about it, but my children turned away from it. They said, no, Daddy. Please, don't tell us about these horrible things. We don't want to know about these things. They are too horrible. But later, I found out, when we had these programs, these Yom HaShoah programs in our synagogue, we had, for instance, one program that we-- every year we changed it-- the-- the-- the theme of the program. So this particular year, we had a theme that said, the second generation speaks, because we wanted to know what the children of survivors say about the Holocaust. How they feel to live in a-- in a home where the parents are survivors. And my daughter, to my surprise-- to my pleasant surprise-- my daughter volunteered. My older daughter volunteered to be one of the speakers. and we had three children of survivors speak on that evening. And I was amazed on what she said and how much she knew about the Holocaust. Without me having told her, they must have researched it on their own or learned about it in school or wherever they found out about it. But they were very well-informed. My younger daughter also was very well-informed about the Holocaust, and I'm glad that they did. And so to this day, I go around and speak about it. And I am glad to do this interview, because now, we realize that we are getting older. It is now 50 years since the time of the liberation, and, look, please, God, maybe we will have another few years to live, but soon, we will not be here, and there will be nobody to speak about this. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Haller, I notice that you are now with-- surrounded by four lovely ladies. And I will let you introduce and tell us who they are and leave it up to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Laurie, and my older daughter, Heidi. And this is my younger daughter, Elizabeth, and my granddaughter, Allison. The two boys, Jonathan and Shawn, are away at camp and they're not here today. My son-in-laws, both John and Nigel, are at work, and they could not be here for this occasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the baby--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, And my-- my younger grandchild, who is only three years old, who is in Virginia. Of course, she could not make it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'm going to ask your daughters to share with us whatever it is that they wish to share in this oral history.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first let me say that I want to thank-- I'm very thankful and grateful for the opportunity to-- to be here and to-- and to hear an oral history of this very important segment of history. And, unfortunately, for me, it's still very, very painful and very hard for me to still deal with any of the facts and the issues of the Holocaust. I think it's very important that people speak about it, and I do everything possible to communicate the facts of the Holocaust to other young people. However for myself personally, it's so difficult for me to even read or go to the movies and actually see visual accounts of the Holocaust because of the feeling that-- the feelings that I have inside of my heart and how personal it is to me. But I-- I really am grateful, and I think that it's so important that we document this history so that people realize that this has happened and so that it can never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I also feel that it's extremely important, and I'm very thankful for this opportunity. I also have a very, very difficult time talking about it and viewing films. But I think that the war against humanity can never be won unless we educate. And I'm very thankful to be here, and I'm very thankful that my father has done this today, because this is a real example of what went on. It's not a textbook, it's real. And the children today who have not been survivors or who have not been touched by prejudice can learn about it in a very, very real sense so that they can feel what went on. It's not a textbook. It's not just in writing. It's very hard to imagine the horror, but when you hear it from somebody that went through it, it's a little easier to sensitize. And it's important that children realize that they have to be tolerant. When they are brought into this world, nobody's prejudiced. They're taught prejudiced. So when-- when they go to school and they're taught to be-- to be tolerant, they don't understand what that means.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by allowing them and-- and giving them the information in this sense, it's real to them. And everyone, everybody in this world, has a job, and that job is to even-- to teach from your experiences and from your heart to other people what justice is and how important that is. So I'm very, very thankful that this-- this day has happened, and I'm very thankful and proud of my father for being able to share the terrible experiences that he's gone through. He's very, very strong to have the strength to endure the physical and emotional pain.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not go out and destruct after this happened to him. I don't know how he managed to control his anger but he has, and he has come forth to be a very, very productive, giving, caring, and loving citizen. And I only wish that more people can learn from this experience, because the human mind is extremely powerful. It could be demented just as easily as it can be excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, Mr.-- Mr. Haller and the Haller family, we want to thank you very much for participating in this Shoah project, and thank you very much once again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 30 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when it was written.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this-- this is the original document that my mother received on January 31, 1939 from the police president in Berlin and telling her that according to the new laws, she has to leave the territory of the Reich within eight weeks. And if she would not do this, she'd be subject to heavy fines and imprisonment. She has the right to make an appeal to this order, but it says here on the bottom, it says, in accordance-- in-- in the public interest, this appeal will be denied. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This document is dated the 28th of December, 1938, and it gives-- it-- it-- it-- it certifies that my mother, Mrs. Anna Haller, is of a good character, and has paid all her taxes, and has no no-- no fines outstanding against her. This also is given by the Department of Internal Revenue. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, this document is also a document by the Internal Revenue Department. It is dated May 4, 1939, which was required to-- for everybody that wanted to leave Germany to show that he has paid all his taxes, including the Judenabgabe, which means the Jewish tax that was imposed after the November 9, 1938, where the Nazis have destroyed all the Jewish properties and have burnt all the synagogues.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish community was fined $1 billion, and everybody had to pay his share of the tax. And in order to leave Germany, you had to show that you did not own-- owe any money, that you have paid all your taxes. And also, it also shows on the name, Anna Sara Haller, that she had to take on the name of Sara. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this also is an authentic document dated May 15, 1939. It was issued by the police precinct to show that Mrs. Anna, Anna Haller, Anna Sara Haller, was of good character and had no-- no fines or anything against her. This document was also needed in order to leave the country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A photocopy of a certificate for-- which was issued to my mother, Anna Haller, on April 25, 1939, which was required for her to-- to-- to leave Germany to state that she was in good health. But as you notice, the doctor was Dr. Herbert Israel Jacob. She had to go to a Jewish doctor who also had to have the surname of Israel. His name was Herbert Jacob, and now his name was Herbert Israel Jacob. Now, Jewish doctors were not allowed to practice medicine to the general public. They were not allowed to treat aliens. They were only allowed to treat Jews. So therefore he had to put on his shingle-- he had to put a Jewish star, and he had to put his full name to indicate that he was a Jewish doctor. The-- the document states that-- that my mother was in good, perfect in physical and mental health. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This-- this is a original star, Jewish star, the yellow star that all Jews were required to-- to wear. Under the Nazi occupation, all Jews were forced to wear a Jewish star. I-- I obtained this star after-- after the war, because my own was, of course, taken away in-- in Auschwitz. All these documents, I obtained after the war from my mother. My mother had taken all these papers to England, and when I visited her there, I found them in her papers. She had all these documents. So I was able to save them, and I have them now with me, and I'm able to show them today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let your daughter see this.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, This picture was taken in 1916, which is a picture of my mother's family. Over here, this is my mother. This is her mother, my grandmother, and her father, my grandfather. This is her older sister, Greta, which moved to Israel. This is her brother, her younger brother, Herman, who died during the Holocaust. He went to the Lodz ghetto. Oh, I mixed it up. This is-- this is him. This is Herman that died in the Holocaust. This one is Morritz. He was the one that had a farm later in New Jersey, where he stayed. This is her younger sister, Bertha, who moved to Israel. And this is her youngest brother, Victor, who just died recently, who lived here in New York. He was the one that I stayed in his apartment when I-- when I came to New York. They're all now passed on, all of them. He was the last one to just pass away last January. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, this is my family, whom I'm very proud of. Here is from-- from right to left, that's my son-in-law, Nigel, my daughter, younger daughter, Elizabeth, this is myself, my wife, my older daughter, Heidi, and her husband, John. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, here is my family again. My granddaughter, Allison, who was here today. My-- her father, Nigel, my son-in-law. This is the other grandson, Jonathan. This is my daughter-- my younger daughter, Elizabeth. And this is Shawn, my younger grandson. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, here's my-- this-- this is my older-- OK, this is my older daughter, Heidi, who is here today. My wife and my son-in-law, her husband, John, and their little daughter, Amanda. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's again, my youngest granddaughter, Amanda, in her home in Virginia. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And this is my oldest grandson, Jonathan, who is not here today. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, well, once again we want to thank you very much for participating in the project and sharing the history that you lived through for history. Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you.
